sections.title: GENERAL REMARKS
sections.text:
6. This Code outlines measures that have been proven to prevent and/or reduce concentrations of hydrogen cyanide in cassava products. When applying the code for cassava processing methods should be carefully chosen from the viewpoint of benefit and feasibility. In addition, these should be implemented in accordance with the relevant national and international legislation and standards.
7. It is recognized that reasonable application of technological measures such as Good Manufacturing Practices (GMP), can be taken to prevent or reduce significantly the concentrations of hydrogen cyanide in cassava products.

sections.title: INTRODUCTION
sections.text:
1. Hydrogen cyanide is a volatile compound which evaporates rapidly in the air at temperatures over 28°C and dissolves rapidly in water. I t may easily be lost during transport, storage and analysis of samples.
2. Hydrogen cyanide is a chemical compound that can be released from cyanogenic glycosides that are natural constituents of some plants such as: bitter almonds, sorghum, cassava, lima beans, stone fruits and bamboo shoots. Therefore reduction and removal measures of hydrogen cyanide ( HCN) should focus on the precursor i.e. cyanogenic glycosides and cyanohydrins.
3. Hydrogen cyanide may be toxic to humans and animals, and the severity of the toxicity depends on the quantity consumed.
4. Cassava is an important staple crop containing cyanogenic glycosides. The cassava plants including the roots also contain the enzyme linamarase that breaks down the cyanogenic glycosides to release cyanohydrin, which dissociates at low levels of acidity to produce hydrogen cyanide. The extent of the breakdown of the cyanogenic glycosides and the eventual release of hydrogen cyanide depends on the amount of linamarase present in the cassava tissue; the extent of the disruption of the tissue, the acidity of the product, and the heat treatment are key factors in determining the concentration of residual cyanogens in cassava products. It is evident that high concentrations of cyanogenic glycosides may result in higher concentrations of hydrogen cyanide.

sections.text:
8. The potential cyanide content in cassava varies with the variety of cassava, the environmental conditions in which it is grown (e.g. drought) and time of harvest.
9. Varieties with low cyanide content have been developed and might be useful in reducing occurrence of hydrogen cyanide in cultivated cassava. Where bitter cassava varieties are used then adequate post harvest processing is essential.
10. Harvesting should be done at the appropriate time because studies have shown increased cyanide in late harvested cassava.

sections.title: TYPICAL PRODUCTION PROCESS
sections.text: 11. Processing is effective in reducing cyanogenic compound content to minimum concentrations when done appropriately. Inadequate or poor processing as sometimes occurs during famine and periods of social stress or the rush to market can lead to high residues of HCN in the final product.  12. The production process for cassava products varies with the intended product. Some examples of cassava products include gari, fufu, cassava flour, cassava starch(tapioca), cassava chips etc. figures 1-8 illustrate the steps in the production processes of some cassava products.

sections.title: Grating the cassava roots
sections.text:
Grating is done either manually by rubbing peeled and washed cassava roots against a metal sheet with perforations made with a nail or mechanically using a grater. During grating, the cyanogenic glycosides are hydrolyzed by the enzyme, linamarase.

the washed cassava roots are cut into small pieces. These will facilitate the fermentation process.

sections.title: Dewatering and Fermentation
sections.text:
i.  In traditional fermentation, fermentation and dewatering are carried out at the same time by packing the grated cassava in sacks and pressed under pressure by putting weights on the sacks or using hydraulic press.  ii. Fermentation is done to develop the taste of the gari. The fermentation period could be between 12 – 24 hours, resulting in the production of gari with an almost bland taste and high starch content, or could vary from 48 – 164 hours resulting in the production of gari with sour taste and lower starch content. iii. During fermentation, especially within 12 – 24 hours, cyanohydrins, which is the intermediate product of the breakdown of the cyanogenic glycoside rapidly dissociates to produce hydrogen cyanide which is volatile and easily lost. However as fermentation is allowed to progress beyond this time, the cassava mash becomes acidic (this is responsible for the sour taste) and the acidity retards the spontaneous dissociation of the cyanohydrins and fixes them in the food. These cyanohydrins slowly dissociate under normal storage conditions; the rate of dissociation is increased by contact with alkalis and/or heat.

Fermentation is carried out in tanks or other suitable fermentation vessels for 3-4 days.

sections.title: Sieving
sections.text:
Sieving is done to remove the large lumps and fibres and also to obtain a homogeneous product for a more uniform roasting of individual particles during the roasting operation.

The fermented cassava pieces are mashed and passed through a sieve, and when the roots are not soft enough to be mashed by hand, they are pounded or passed through a grater before the fibres are removed by adding water to the mash and filtering.

sections.title: Roasting
sections.text: Should be properly done by placing the sieved fermented grated cassava on a pan stirring until it becomes dry. Palm oil may be added during roasting as is done in some parts of Nigeria. Roasting has an effect on the amount of residual cyanogenic compound in the final product and the shelf life/storability of the product.

sections.title: Soaking
sections.text: Soaking in water is often done for one to three (1 -3) days, before or after the chipping operation during which some fermentation takes place that gives the chips the sour flavour favoured by some consumers. It also allows hydrogen cyanide to diffuse out making the product safer for human consumption. The National Root Crop Research Institute in Nigeria suggested that optimal hydrocyanic acid reduction can be achieved through a combination of 15 minute soaking and 2 minute blanching of cassava chips.

sections.title: GENERAL RECOMMENDATIONS
sections.text: National, state and local governments as well as non-governmental organizations (NGOs, commercial associations and cooperatives) should be involved in promoting effective cassava cultivation with the introduction of low cyanide, high yielding and well-adapted varieties of cassava and processing methods as a means to ensure maximum reduction of residual cyanogens in cassava food products. Campaigns for introduction of other staples, vegetables, pulses and fruits to decrease the daily cyanide intake and broaden the diet could also result in lower consumption of cyanogenic glycosides.

pages.title: CODE OF PRACTICE FOR THE REDUCTION OF HYDROCYANIC ACID (HCN) IN CASSAVA AND CASSAVA PRODUCTS (CXC 73-2013)
pages.summary: A página contém o título principal do documento e um cabeçalho de página repetitivo. A estrutura hierárquica foi dividida em quatro seções principais: INTRODUCTION, SCOPE, GENERAL REMARKS e MEASURES TO REDUCE THE PRECUSOR OF HYDROGEN CYANIDE, todas como nível 1. Cada seção contém parágrafos associados como texto interno. A última seção continua na próxima página, indicando conteúdo incompleto nesta página para RAG.

A página contém o cabeçalho repetitivo 'CXC 73-2013 Page 2 of 14'. Identifica-se dois títulos principais de nível 1: 'TYPICAL PRODUCTION PROCESS' e 'GARI PRODUCTION'. 'TYPICAL PRODUCTION PROCESS' inclui os parágrafos 11 e 12 associados ao texto da página. 'GARI PRODUCTION' é uma seção que começa com o parágrafo 13 e detalha em subtítulos enumerados de 'a' a 'g' as etapas do processo, cada uma com seu texto explicativo. A seção 'GARI PRODUCTION' continua para a próxima página, indicado pelo 'continues_on_next_page': true. Os subtítulos são todos nível 2 sob 'GARI PRODUCTION'. Não há indicação de continuidade da página anterior para estas seções nesta página.

A página contém três títulos principais em caixa alta, não numerados, classificados como nível 1: 'FUFU AND FUFU POWDER PRODUCTION', 'DRIED CASSAVA CHIPS', e 'OTHER CASSAVA PRODUCTS'. Cada título principal apresenta subtítulos enumerados de 'a' a 'h' ou 'a' a 'c' conforme a seção, todos nível 2, com textos associados detalhando processos de produção ou etapas. A seção 'OTHER CASSAVA PRODUCTS' indica continuar na próxima página, marcada pela propriedade 'continues_on_next_page': true em seu nó principal e nos subtítulos. Não há indicação de conteúdo vindo da página anterior para estes nós nesta página.

A página contém três títulos principais em caixa alta, classificados como nível 1: 'PRACTICES BASED ON GOOD AGRICULTURAL PRACTICES', 'RECOMMENDED PRACTICES BASED ON GOOD MANUFACTURING PROCESSES' e 'PREPARATION OF CASSAVA PRODUCTS', cada um com subtítulos enumerados conforme o texto. Os textos associados descrevem práticas e processos detalhados. Não há indicações de continuidade ou conteúdo vindo de página anterior nesta página.

A página contém listagem enumerada de práticas (26 e 27) com texto explicativo detalhado para cada, seguidas pelo título em caixa alta 'GENERAL RECOMMENDATIONS' sem numeração, que agrega recomendações gerais. Os textos são associados corretamente e não há indicações de continuidade ou conteúdo vindo de página anterior.
Página 6 contém duas recomendações enumeradas (40 e 41) com seus textos explicativos. Não há título principal nem seção agrupadora visível. Não há continuidade de conteúdo para outras páginas.
Página 7 contém apenas um cabeçalho repetitivo e figura ilustrativa sem estrutura de títulos ou texto explicativo. Não há nós hierárquicos identificados para extração.
